summary: Venetian Renaissance painter. Known for Venus of Urbino, Rape of Europa, Equestrian Portrait of Charles V, poesie paintings for Philip II (including Diana and Actaeon, Danaë), The Flaying of Marsyas, and numerous portraits. Student of Giorgione (collaborated on Pastoral Concert). Court painter to the Habsburgs.

works.name: Venus of Urbino (1538)
works.description: Reclining nude on a bed with a sleeping dog; a maid rummages through a chest in the background. Inspired by Giorgione's Sleeping Venus. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Reclining nude woman on a bed; a maid rummages through a chest behind a sleeping dog

a maid looking in a chest behind a bed where a little dog sleeps near a reclining nude woman

clues.clue: Inspired by Giorgione's painting of a nude reclining against a red pillow
examples: A painting by this artist's teacher that depicts a nude reclining against a red pillow likely inspired him

works.name: Poesie Paintings for Philip II (Rape of Europa, Danaë, Diana and Actaeon, etc.)
works.description: Series of mythological paintings for King Philip II of Spain, called 'poesie.' Wikipedia

Habsburg patrons also commissioned his Rape of Europa

clues.clue: Danaë — a maid watches a gold cloud approach the reclining nude; first version for a Farnese, later version for Philip II
examples: this artist's 'poesie' paintings include one where a maid watches a gold cloud approach Danae

clues.clue: Diana and Actaeon — two-part depiction of the myth for Philip II
examples: this artist made a two-part depiction of the Diana and Actaeon story

works.name: Equestrian Portrait of Charles V (1548)
works.description: Depicts Emperor Charles V in full armor on horseback at the Battle of Mühlberg, despite the subject being gouty. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Depicts a gouty man in full armor carrying a spear and riding a horse at the Battle of Mühlberg
examples: unrealistically depicted a gouty man in full armor, carrying a spear and riding a horse at the Battle of Muhlberg
massive Equestrian Portrait of Charles V

works.name: The Flaying of Marsyas
works.description: Late work. A dog laps blood being caught in a bucket; contains a self-portrait as Midas watching Apollo kneel beside the upside-down Marsyas.

clues.clue: A dog laps blood being caught in a bucket; self-portrait as Midas watching Apollo kneel beside the upside-down title figure
examples: A bucket is being brought for the blood being lapped by a dog; contains a self-portrait as Midas watching Apollo kneel beside the upside-down title figure

works.name: Portrait of a Man with a Blue Sleeve / Man with a Quilted Sleeve
works.description: Portrait in which the subject leans a blue quilted sleeve over a parapet. Vasari incorrectly claimed it depicted the poet Ariosto. Quoted by Rembrandt's Self-portrait at the Age of 40.

clues.clue: Subject leans blue quilted sleeve over a parapet; Vasari incorrectly claimed it depicted Ariosto; quoted by Rembrandt
examples: a bearded young man looks sidelong at the viewer and rests his right elbow on a parapet while wearing a huge blue sleeve
Rembrandt's Self-portrait at the Age of 40 quotes a painting by this artist that Vasari incorrectly claims depicts the poet Ariosto

comprehensive_summary:
Titian (Tiziano Vecellio, c. 1488-1576) was the leading Venetian Renaissance painter. His most commonly clued work is Venus of Urbino (1538), depicting a reclining nude on a bed with a sleeping dog while a maid rummages through a chest, inspired by his teacher Giorgione's Sleeping Venus.

His 'poesie' paintings for King Philip II of Spain include Rape of Europa (a cherub rides a fish as Europa clings to the horn of a white bull), Danaë (a maid watches a gold cloud approach the reclining nude), and Diana and Actaeon. The Equestrian Portrait of Charles V (1548) unrealistically depicts the gouty emperor in full armor on horseback at the Battle of Mühlberg. The Flaying of Marsyas is a late masterpiece featuring a self-portrait as Midas watching Apollo flay the upside-down Marsyas, with a dog lapping blood.

His portrait of a man with a blue quilted sleeve (Man with a Quilted Sleeve) was incorrectly attributed by Vasari as depicting Ariosto and was quoted by Rembrandt. He portrayed Isabella d'Este at age 62 as a baby-faced young woman. His Allegory of Prudence shows heads of a dog, lion, and wolf beneath heads of three men (thought to be Titian, his son Orazio, and cousin Marco). He was a student of Giorgione and collaborated on Pastoral Concert.
